hero.badge: Wellness that starts with an assessment
hero.sub: Take a short assessment. Get a report made just for you. Then follow a daily audio plan, ebooks, and counselling built around your results. Just ten quiet minutes a day.
hero.ratingCalmer: feel calmer in the morning by day 14
hero.imageAlt: A calm person sitting at a tidy desk with a hand on their heart, next to a plant, a cup of tea, an open journal and books

journey.lede: At Daybreak, it all starts with understanding yourself and everything after is built on that.

steps.title: Take your assessment
steps.text: Pick a topic that feels hard right now. Answer honestly. It takes about 12 quiet minutes.

steps.title: See your report
steps.text: Your scores in each area, explained in plain words: what they mean for you, and what to do next.

steps.title: Do it daily
steps.text: A 7–30 day audio plan built from your report. One session opens each day. Five to ten minutes.

steps.title: Take it again & see the change
steps.text: After 60–90 days, take the assessment again and see your before and after side by side.

journey.loopNote: Then the path repeats a deeper topic, a new plan, and progress you can see.

ecosystem.lede: Once you have your report, everything else can be shaped around it and the music is free for everyone, always.
ecosystem.ebookTitle: A short book with your name on the cover
ecosystem.ebookText: Made from your report about your scores, your habits, and your next 30 days. General books are open to everyone.
ecosystem.ebookMockTitle: Quiet the Noise: Maya’s 30 Days to Recover
ecosystem.voiceText: A talking helper that has already read your report, so you never start from “tell me about yourself.”
ecosystem.musicText: Press play right now. No paywall, no sign-up just somewhere calm to start.

proof.h2em: when you can see it change.
proof.progressFoot: Same questions, same scoring, so the change you see is real, not a change in the test.

testimonials.quote: The report put words to something I couldn’t. “Your energy isn’t broken, your rest is.”  I think about that line every night.
Ten minutes a day felt too small to matter. Then I took the assessment again and my numbers went up.
testimonials.role: Sleep & Rest path · +27 the second time

cta.text: Twelve minutes of honest answers today. A clearer picture of yourself tomorrow and a path that stays with you after that.
cta.secondary: Just the free music, for now
cta.referral: It helps to have a friend along. Invite one and you both get a free session.

report.lede: Not just a number and a pat on the back — a full picture of where you are, written like a kind, honest friend.

points.title: Plain words, no jargon
points.text: Every score is explained for you — what it means, why it shows up, and what usually helps.

points.title: Consistent and reliable
points.text: Your results use a fixed scoring method, so the same answers always give the same result. No AI guessing.

points.title: It shapes everything after
points.text: Your audio plan, your ebook, and your counselling sessions are all built from this one report.

quote: Maya, your exhaustion is high and your energy to recover is running low —
your energy isn’t broken, your rest is.
Your 14-day plan starts there: evenings first.
Jonas, your wind-down really works — it’s staying regular that slips.
and let bedtime meet it. You can sleep better.
Aisha, you read other people really well — empathy is a real strength.

audioPlan.lede: Your report builds a day-by-day plan of calming sessions, picked and ordered for your scores. One opens each morning, so it becomes a habit, not a binge.
audioPlan.trailAria: Your 14-day plan — 3 days done, day 4 is today, the rest open one each morning
audioPlan.moodNote: How your mood is going over the plan
audioPlan.welcome: “Hi Maya, this plan starts with your evenings, because that’s where your report says rest slips away…”
audioPlan.sessionMeta: Stress & Burnout path · 7 min · evening session

assess.lede: Careful, well-made assessments thoughtful questions scored across real areas, not a quick five-question quiz. Twenty topics, one honest place to start.
assess.bundleText: Take linked assessments like Stress & Burnout → Emotional Intelligence together and save 30%.

advisor.blurb: Pick whatever’s on your mind and talk it through out loud with an AI advisor you actually speak to. Anyone can, no report needed. Have one? It just helps Sol get to know you better.

stress.title: Stress & Pressure
stress.line: When everything feels like too much at once.

anxiety.title: Anxiety & Worry
anxiety.line: When your mind races ahead to what might go wrong.

sleep.title: Sleep & Rest
sleep.line: Tired but wired nights, heavy mornings.

feelings.title: Understanding My Feelings
feelings.line: Naming what’s going on inside, and why.

focus.title: Focus & Overwhelm
focus.line: Scattered days that end busy but blurry.

relationships.title: Relationships
relationships.line: The people close to you, and the tension.

self-worth.title: Self-Worth
self-worth.line: The voice that says you’re not enough.

direction.title: Work & Direction
direction.line: Feeling stuck, or unsure what’s next.

grief.title: Grief & Loss
grief.line: Carrying something, or someone, you’ve lost.

open.title: Something on my mind
open.line: Not sure how to name it yet? Just start talking.

takeaways: Your recovery, not your effort, is the weakest link right now.
One clear limit tonight: charge your phone outside the bedroom from 9pm.
Pair it with Day 4 of your audio plan, which works on the same evening time.
lines: Hi Maya. I’ve got your Stress & Burnout report to go on, but this is your time, so take it wherever you need. How are you feeling today?
Honestly? Tired. The kind sleep doesn’t seem to fix.
That fits what I’m seeing. Your exhaustion has eased since your first check-in, but recovery is still the weakest link. What do your evenings usually look like?
Work spills into them. I’m answering messages until I fall asleep.
So the day never really ends. That’s what we want to change, not by doing more, but by protecting the last hour. What’s one small thing you could let go of tonight?
Maybe… not checking email after dinner.

That’s a real limit. Charge your phone in another room from 9pm. Just for tonight, notice how it feels. And there’s a short chapter from your scores about exactly this. I can add it to your shelf after.

takeaways: Your wind-down works. It’s doing it every night that slips.
Set one fixed wake-up time, and let your bedtime follow.
Move worry to an 8pm slot, so bed stops being where you sort things out.
lines: Evening, Maya. I’ve read your Sleep & Rest report, so I’ve got the background, but talk to me about whatever’s really on your mind. How have the nights been?
Falling asleep is fine. Staying asleep is the problem.
Your scores hint at that. Your wind-down is good, but your routine slips. When you wake at 3am, where does your mind tend to go?
Straight to tomorrow. Everything I haven’t done.
Then let’s give that thinking an earlier home. A five-minute worry time at 8pm, written down and set aside, so bed isn’t where you plan.
I could try that.
Good. Tonight’s audio session is built around exactly this wind-down. Want me to line it up for after we hang up?

feelings.suggestTitle: Retake it to see how you’ve grown
takeaways: You read others really well. Turning that inward is where you can grow.
Practice: one honest “I feel…” sentence a day, easy ones first.
Calming yourself grows fastest. Feet on the floor, one breath, then speak.
lines: Hi Maya. I already know your EQ profile, so we can skip the basics and go wherever you like. What’s really on your mind today?
I notice everyone else’s mood but I lose track of my own.
That’s exactly what your scores show: high empathy, lower self-awareness. The good news: it’s the easiest gap to train. Can you name what you’re feeling right now, in one word?
Umm… stretched.
A perfect start. You found it in seconds. Three times a day, just ask yourself that one question. No fixing, only naming. It changes things faster than you’d think.
And when you want to see the change in numbers, a retake in a few weeks will show it.

generic.suggestAssessment: Take the {{topic}} assessment to learn more
generic.suggestFallback: Find the right place to start
generic.takeaway1Open: Putting words to what’s been sitting with you is already a real step.
generic.takeaway1Named: You named {{topic}} as the thing to look at, and that’s already a step.
generic.takeaway2: Small, regular steps help more than big, now-and-then efforts.
generic.takeaway3Assessment: A short {{topic}} assessment would turn this into something clear to track.
generic.takeaway3None: We can keep talking it through. No plan needed. The way can show itself.
generic.line1Open: Hi Maya. No topic, no report, let’s just talk. What’s been on your mind lately?
generic.line1Named: Hi Maya. We don’t have a report on {{topic}} yet, so I’ll get to know it as we talk. No pressure. What brings you here today?
generic.line2: I’ve been carrying it a while. I’m not even sure where to start.
generic.line3: That’s okay. Not knowing where to start is a fine place to begin. When it’s loudest, is it more in your body, your thoughts, or your energy?
generic.line4: Mostly in my head. It just loops.
generic.line5: Then let’s give the loop somewhere to land. In one sentence, what worries you most about this?
generic.line6: That I’ll keep going like this and nothing really changes.
generic.line7Assessment: That fear can be worked on, and it gets clearer with detail. A short {{topic}} assessment would turn this vague weight into something clear we can actually move. Want me to line one up?
generic.line7None: That fear can be worked on. Change rarely comes all at once. It’s one honest choice, made again and again. What’s the smallest one you could make before we talk again?

assessment.a: Any topic you pick, like Stress & Burnout, Sleep, or Emotional Intelligence. Your plan gives you that many full assessments (with reports and audio plans) each month.

runOut.q: What happens when I run out?
runOut.aStripe: Nothing stops working. After that, you just pay for each item you want — an extra assessment, ebook, or counselling minutes, one at a time.
runOut.aMsisdn: With mobile billing, you can’t buy extras one by one. You just wait for your count to reset at the start of next month. Anything you already unlocked stays yours.

change.q: Can I change or cancel my plan?
change.a: Anytime, on your billing page. A bigger plan starts right away. A smaller plan or a cancel takes effect at the end of the month.

carry.q: Do unused items carry over to next month?
carry.a: No. Your count resets each month. But anything you already unlocked is yours to keep.

stress-burnout.title: Stress & Burnout
stress-burnout.tagline: For when life looks fine on the outside but feels heavy inside.
stress-burnout.blurb: A clear look at where your energy goes, and why rest stops helping. We look at four parts of your life, score your honest answers, and build a plan that starts where you feel it most.

overall.low: Your overall load is in a healthy range. The parts below show where pressure builds up first for you. Your plan works on keeping those clear.
overall.mid: You are under real pressure and still standing. But some things that should refill you are running low. Caught now, this turns around fast.
overall.high: Your load is high in several parts at once. None of this is a judgment. High scores just show where your plan begins, and these numbers move with practice.

plan.welcome: this plan starts with your evenings, because that’s where your answers say your rest slips away…

exhaustion.label: Exhaustion
exhaustion.desc: How low your energy is, in body and mind.
exhaustion.rec: Set one quiet wind-down time before 9pm, every day for two weeks. Do not skip it.

hints.low: Your energy is holding steady. That is a real strength, and your plan will help you keep it.
hints.mid: Your energy dips, but not too much yet. The goal now is to protect the hours that refill you, before the dips get worse.
hints.high: Your energy is running on empty. The first step is to do less, not more. Your plan starts by taking things off your plate.

cynicism.label: Feeling distant
cynicism.desc: How far you feel from the things that used to matter to you.
cynicism.rec: Once a day, name one task that still feels worth doing, before you open anything else.

hints.low: You still feel connected to what you do. That helps you bounce back faster.
hints.mid: You are starting to feel a bit distant. This is usually a way to protect yourself, not a change in who you are. It eases as the pressure drops.
hints.high: Feeling distant has become your way to cope. That numb feeling is a sign. It shows where things have been too heavy for too long.

control.label: Sense of control
control.desc: How much say you feel you have over your time, speed, and choices.
control.rec: Each morning, pick your first task yourself, before email picks it for you.

hints.low: You feel in charge of your days. Keep noticing the small choices you make. That sense of control grows.
hints.mid: Your days are partly yours and partly your inbox’s. Taking back even one hour you choose will move this score fast.
hints.high: Right now your days feel like they just happen to you. Your plan rebuilds your sense of control in tiny steps. Minutes, not big life changes.

recovery.label: Recovery capacity
recovery.desc: How well your rest really refreshes you.
recovery.rec: Swap your last 20 minutes of screen time for one audio session from your plan.

hints.low: Your rest really refreshes you. That is rare, and worth protecting.
hints.mid: Your rest works, but not every time. The fix is usually a steady routine, not more hours.
hints.high: Your rest is not reaching you. Evenings pass, weekends pass, and you still feel empty. This is where your plan starts: evenings first.

questions: I wake up tired, even after a full night of sleep.
By mid-afternoon, I have little energy left for the things I care about.
Small tasks feel heavier than they should.
I feel distant or like I don’t care about things that used to matter to me.
I catch myself thinking “what’s the point?” about daily tasks.
My days feel like they just happen to me, instead of me shaping them.
I can choose the speed and order of my day.
Evenings and weekends really recharge me.
Even when I rest, my mind keeps working.
I keep time in my week that is just for rest.

sleep-rest.title: Sleep & Rest
sleep-rest.tagline: For nights when you’re tired but wired, and mornings that start off rough.
sleep-rest.blurb: A look at four parts of your nights: sleep pressure, winding down, your routine, and the worry that shows up when the lights go out. Scored honestly, turned into a nightly plan.

overall.low: Your sleep is basically working. The parts below are early warning signs. Your plan keeps them in the clear.
overall.mid: Your nights are strained in a few places you can fix. Sleep improves fast with a steady routine. Most people feel the change within two weeks.
overall.high: Your sleep is strained in several parts. The good news: sleep responds better than almost anything to small, repeated changes.

plan.welcome: we start tonight. Not with rules, but with a gentle wind-down your body can learn…

debt.label: Sleep pressure
debt.desc: How much built-up tiredness you carry into each day.
debt.rec: Go to bed 20 minutes earlier, just 20, for the next ten nights.

hints.low: You’re running on a mostly full tank. You’re getting about the sleep you need.
hints.mid: You’re building up a small sleep debt. It’s not huge, but it adds up. Small, regular catch-ups work best.
hints.high: Your body badly needs to catch up. The plan aims for steady, slightly earlier nights instead of big weekend catch-ups.

winddown.label: Wind-down
winddown.desc: How well your evenings help you switch from doing to resting.
winddown.rec: Pick a set time each evening to lower the lights. The audio session marks it.

hints.low: Your evenings already slow down well. Your sessions will build on what’s working.
hints.mid: Your wind-down works when you let it. The gap is doing it every night, not the method itself.
hints.high: You go from full speed to stopped with nothing in between. Your plan builds a 30-minute wind-down your body can learn.

rhythm.label: A steady routine
rhythm.desc: How steady your sleep and wake times are during the week.
rhythm.rec: Set your wake-up time first. Let your bedtime follow.

hints.low: Your body clock keeps a steady beat. Protect it. It’s quietly doing a lot for you.
hints.mid: Your routine slips on weekends and busy weeks. Just keeping one fixed wake-up time steadies the rest.
hints.high: Your body clock gets mixed signals every day. One fixed wake-up time, even on weekends, is the single biggest change you can make.

nightworry.label: Worry at night
nightworry.desc: How much your mind starts racing when the lights go out.
nightworry.rec: Take 5 minutes at 8pm to write down your worries, then set them aside.

hints.low: Your mind is mostly quiet at night. The plan keeps it that way with a little upkeep.
hints.mid: Worry shows up some nights. Writing it down, instead of holding it in bed, usually helps within a week.
hints.high: Nighttime has become when your mind works hardest. Your plan moves worry to earlier in the evening, so bed stops being the place you sort things out.

questions: I need an alarm, and the snooze button, to get up when I mean to.
I feel properly rested when I wake up.
I use screens right up until I try to sleep.
My evenings have a clear point where I start slowing down.
My sleep and wake times change by more than an hour during the week.
I get up at about the same time on weekends.
When the lights go out, my mind gets louder.
I go over conversations or plan tomorrow while trying to fall asleep.
I need caffeine to get through a normal day.
I fall asleep within twenty minutes most nights.

anxiety-worry.title: Anxiety & Worry
anxiety-worry.tagline: For when your mind jumps ahead to things that haven’t happened.
anxiety-worry.blurb: Where your worry sits, how it shows up in your body, what it makes you avoid, and how easily you can come back to the present. Mapped gently, scored clearly.

overall.low: Your worry mostly stays in proportion. It’s there, but not running the show. The map below shows where it builds up when it does rise.
overall.mid: Worry has taken real hold in some places, while other strengths stay intact. Your plan works on the weak spots and builds on the strengths.
overall.high: Worry is loud in several areas right now. That’s tiring, and it can be worked on. These four parts respond well to short, daily, physical practice.

plan.welcome: we won’t fight your worries. We’ll practise coming back from them, a little each day…

rumination.label: Going in circles
rumination.desc: How much your mind loops on the same worries without solving them.
rumination.rec: When a loop starts, say it out loud once, like “planning again,” then get up and move.

hints.low: Your thoughts mostly pass through and move on. That’s worth noticing. It’s a skill you already have.
hints.mid: Some thoughts get stuck on repeat. Loops weaken when you catch them early. Your sessions practise exactly that.
hints.high: Your mind has worn deep grooves of the same thoughts. Your plan doesn’t argue with the loops. It teaches you to step off the track.

body.label: Body signals
body.desc: How strongly worry shows up in your body: chest, breath, stomach, jaw.
body.rec: Twice a day, breathe out longer than you breathe in, for ten breaths. That’s the whole exercise.

hints.low: Your body stays mostly calm under stress. That’s a strong base for everything else.
hints.mid: Your body warns you before your mind notices. Learning its early signals turns them into helpful alerts.
hints.high: Your body is holding the worry your mind won’t put down. Your plan starts with the body: breath and muscles first, thoughts second.

avoidance.label: Avoidance
avoidance.desc: How much worry has started to shrink what you’re willing to do.
avoidance.rec: List three things you’ve stopped doing. Pick the easiest. Do a little of it this week.

hints.low: Worry isn’t making your choices. Your world is staying full-sized. Keep it that way.
hints.mid: A few doors have quietly closed. Opening them again in small steps is the most reliable way to work on anxiety.
hints.high: Worry has been making your world smaller each month. Your plan turns this around gently, one small step at a time.

grounding.label: Grounding
grounding.desc: How easily you can come back to the present when worry spikes.
grounding.rec: Practise the 5-4-3-2-1 senses exercise once a day, while calm, so it works when you’re not.

hints.low: You can find your way back to the present. Sessions will make it second nature.
hints.mid: You can steady yourself when you remember to. The practice is making it automatic.
hints.high: When worry spikes, the present is hard to reach. Your plan drills short, physical ways to steady yourself until they’re there when you need them.

questions: I keep going over worries long after I can do anything about them.
My mind jumps to the worst version of everyday situations.
I notice tension in my chest, jaw, or stomach during normal days.
My breathing stays slow and easy under pressure.
I put off tasks, calls, or places because of how they might make me feel.
I say yes to things even when they make me nervous.
When worry spikes, I can bring myself back to the present.
Once anxious, I stay anxious for hours.
I lose sleep going over things that might go wrong.
My body feels restless even when nothing urgent is happening.

focus-clarity.title: Focus & Clarity
focus-clarity.tagline: For scattered days that end busy, blurry, and strangely empty.
focus-clarity.blurb: Where your focus leaks, what overloads it, when your mind is sharpest, and how easily you can get real, deep work done right now. A clear map of a foggy thing.

overall.low: Your focus is basically solid. The parts below show where to build, not fix.
overall.mid: Your focus is leaking in clear places. Focus can be trained, and leaks this size usually close within one plan.
overall.high: The fog is thick right now. Your focus is scattered, your head is overloaded, and deep work feels out of reach. All four of these can be trained, and your plan trains them daily.

plan.welcome: we begin with one small block of quiet time. Small enough to keep, real enough to matter…

distraction.label: Pull of distractions
distraction.desc: How strongly interruptions and devices pull you off task.
distraction.rec: One 25-minute block daily: phone in another room. Not silenced. Another room.

hints.low: Your focus holds well. The sessions will sharpen a skill you already have.
hints.mid: You’re losing focus in small, frequent leaks rather than big breaks. Fixing two or three of them changes whole days.
hints.high: You’re interrupted so often you can barely focus at all. Your plan rebuilds it the only way that works: short blocks of quiet time, daily.

overload.label: Too much in your head
overload.desc: How much you’re keeping in your head instead of written down.
overload.rec: Each night, take 3 minutes to write down everything on your mind before sleep.

hints.low: Your head is mostly clear. Your notes and lists are holding what they should.
hints.mid: You’re holding more in your head than you need to. Every thing you write down clears your mind a little.
hints.high: Your mind is trying to store everything, remember everything, and remind you of everything at once. Your plan starts by getting it all out of your head and onto paper.

energy.label: Your energy pattern
energy.desc: How well your work lines up with the times your mind is actually sharp.
energy.rec: Find your sharpest 90 minutes. Put your hardest thing there. Defend it.

hints.low: You mostly spend your best hours on your best work. That’s rarer than you’d think.
hints.mid: Some of your sharpest hours go to your most boring tasks. Swapping even one hour helps right away.
hints.high: Your best hours go to email and meetings, leaving only fog for what matters. Your plan takes back one good hour at a time.

deepwork.label: Getting into deep focus
deepwork.desc: How easily you can get into, and stay in, deep focus.
deepwork.rec: Same desk, same playlist, same first step. Make starting a routine, not a decision.

hints.low: You can still get into deep focus when you choose to. That skill grows. Use it on purpose.
hints.mid: Deep focus comes, but it takes a long warm-up and breaks easily. A set start routine gets you there faster.
hints.high: Deep focus has become rare. It’s not gone, it’s just out of practice. Your plan rebuilds your start routine from scratch.

questions: I check my phone within minutes of starting focused work.
I can work for thirty minutes without switching tasks.
I keep important to-dos in my head rather than written down.
I feel behind on things I can’t even name.
My clearest hours go to meetings, email, or admin.
I save hard work for the time of day I focus best.
I can sink into a task deeply enough to lose track of time.
Even simple tasks take me far longer than they should.
Notifications decide the order of my day.
My mind feels like a browser with forty tabs open.

emotional-intelligence.title: Emotional Intelligence
emotional-intelligence.tagline: For understanding your own feelings, and reading them in others.
emotional-intelligence.blurb: Four skills, measured honestly: how well you notice your own feelings, calm them, read others, and say what’s true. Here, higher scores are strengths, and the report shows where to grow.

overall.low: These skills are just getting started for you, which is when they grow the fastest. Every part below can be trained, and the plan trains them in order.
overall.mid: You have a real base here, with clear room to grow. The report shows exactly which skill to work on next for the biggest change.
overall.high: These skills are strong across the board. The work now is staying steady under pressure, keeping them going when it’s hard.

plan.welcome: we’ll build four skills in order. Noticing first, because everything else builds on it…
sessions: One word for how you feel

awareness.label: Self-awareness
awareness.desc: How well you notice and name what you’re feeling, as it happens.
awareness.rec: Three times a day, one question: “What am I feeling, in one word?” That’s it.

hints.low: Feelings tend to reach you late, as moods, tension, or sudden reactions. Practising naming them changes this faster than any other skill here.
hints.mid: You catch your bigger feelings well, but the smaller ones still slip past. Your sessions help you notice the subtle ones.
hints.high: You notice your own feelings early and clearly. That’s the base every other skill builds on.

regulation.label: Calming yourself
regulation.desc: How well you can calm a strong feeling without pushing it away.
regulation.rec: When things heat up: feel your feet on the floor, take one slow breath, then speak. The order matters.

hints.low: Right now strong feelings are in charge and you go along for the ride. Calming yourself is the most trainable skill here, and your plan builds it gently.
hints.mid: You can calm yourself, but it takes effort and a bit of time. Practice makes it faster.
hints.high: You can hold a strong feeling without it taking over. Others can feel how steady you are.

empathy.label: Empathy
empathy.desc: How well you read what others are feeling, under what they say.
empathy.rec: Once a day, quietly guess what someone is feeling, then gently check if you can.

hints.low: Other people’s feelings often catch you by surprise. Staying curious, every day, is the whole fix.
hints.mid: You read people well when you’re present, but stress narrows your view. The work is keeping it on when it counts.
hints.high: You pick up on what isn’t said. Used with care, that’s a rare and valuable skill.

expression.label: Speaking up
expression.desc: How clearly and safely you can say what you really feel and need.
expression.rec: Once a day, say one honest sentence that starts with “I feel…” to anyone, about anything.

hints.low: What’s true for you tends to stay inside, or come out the wrong way. Small, low-pressure moments of honesty build this faster than big talks.
hints.mid: You say what you feel when it’s easy, but the important talks still get put off. Your sessions practise exactly those.
hints.high: You can say hard, honest things in a way people can hear. That’s the top skill, and you have it.

questions: I can name what I’m feeling while I’m feeling it.
I notice my moods only after they’ve affected my whole day.
When something upsets me, I can calm myself within minutes.
I say or send things in the heat of a moment that I later regret.
I sense how someone is feeling before they tell me.
People’s reactions often catch me completely off guard.
I can tell people what I need without it turning into a fight.
I keep my real feelings in until they come out the wrong way.
I understand why I reacted the way I did, soon after.
Stress makes me react more strongly than I mean to.

exhaustion.blurb: How depleted you feel — physically and mentally — by the demands of your days.

note.low: Your tank is reading fairly full. Energy is showing up when you need it.
note.mid: You’re running warmer than is comfortable — noticeable tiredness that rest isn’t fully clearing.
note.high: You’re depleted often. This is the body asking for real recovery, not just a weekend.

bullets: Tiredness that lingers past a normal night’s sleep is a load signal, not a character flaw.
Exhaustion compounds quietly — small recovery habits matter more than one big reset.
exhaustion.try: Pick one fixed “off switch” tonight — screens down 30 minutes before bed — and protect it for a week.

cynicism.blurb: How connected — or detached and going-through-the-motions — you feel about your work and people.

note.low: You’re still engaged and present. The work and people around you still land as meaningful.
note.mid: Some distance is creeping in — moments of “why bother” or going through the motions.
note.high: You’re feeling detached a lot. Pulling back is a normal way the mind protects itself under strain.

bullets: Cynicism is often a symptom of depletion, not your true outlook — it usually softens as reserves return.
Naming one thing that still matters to you re-opens a little of the connection.
cynicism.try: Each evening, note one small moment that felt genuinely worthwhile. No pressure for it to be big.

workload.blurb: How much the sheer volume and pace of demands outstrips the time and resources you have.

note.low: The volume feels manageable. You generally have room to do things at a sustainable pace.
note.mid: The load is bumping the ceiling — frequent stretches where there’s more to do than time to do it.
note.high: Demands are routinely outrunning your capacity. This is a structural pressure, not a willpower gap.

bullets: Sustained overload is the single strongest driver of burnout — it’s worth treating as a real constraint.
Protecting capacity often means subtracting commitments, not just working harder inside them.
workload.try: Name the one task that drains the most for the least return — and ask what it would take to drop or hand it off.

recovery.blurb: How well you’re able to switch off, rest, and genuinely recharge between demands.

note.low: Recovery is running thin. You’re rarely fully switching off, so the tank isn’t refilling.
note.mid: You get some recovery, but it’s patchy — rest that doesn’t quite reach the bottom of the tank.
note.high: You recover well. You can put things down and come back genuinely refreshed — a real asset.

bullets: Recovery is a skill and a resource, not a reward for finishing — it works best scheduled, not earned.
Even short, deliberate breaks protect you more than a single long collapse at the end.
recovery.try: Block one genuinely off-duty pocket this week — phone elsewhere — and treat it as non-negotiable.

control.blurb: How much agency and say you feel you have over how your days and work unfold.

note.low: Agency is feeling thin — a lot of your day shaped by forces you don’t feel you can steer.
note.mid: You have some say, but not as much as you’d like — pockets of feeling pushed along by events.
note.high: You feel a real sense of agency. Knowing you can steer your days is protective under pressure.

bullets: Even small, reclaimed choices restore a sense of control that buffers against strain.
Control is less about changing everything and more about owning the few levers you do have.
control.try: Choose one small thing this week you’ll decide on your own terms — when you start, what you say no to.

runningOnEmpty.name: Running on Empty
runningOnEmpty.summary: High demands, low recovery — a classic burnout pattern, and a reversible one.

stretchedButResourced.name: Stretched but Resourced
stretchedButResourced.summary: You’re carrying a lot, but you still have real ways to recharge.

coastingOnLowReserves.name: Coasting on Low Reserves
coastingOnLowReserves.summary: Not heavily strained yet, but your reserves are thin — an early-warning pattern worth tending.

balancedAndSteady.name: Balanced & Steady
balancedAndSteady.summary: Your demands and recovery are in a healthy balance right now.

patternDetail.base: Your demands are running at {{difficulty}}/100 and your capacity to recover sits at {{resource}}/100. {{connector}}{{heaviest}} is adding the most pressure, while {{thinnest}} is the resource most worth rebuilding.
patternDetail.demandsHeavier: The demands side is clearly the heavier of the two right now —
patternDetail.reserveHigher: You’ve got more in reserve than is being asked of you at the moment —
patternDetail.evenlyMatched: The two are fairly evenly matched —

sleeplessness.blurb: How much trouble you have falling asleep, staying asleep, or settling a busy mind at night.

note.low: Sleep is coming fairly easily — you’re falling and staying asleep without much of a fight.
note.mid: Sleep is bumpy — some nights you lie awake or wake and struggle to drop back off.
note.high: You’re wrestling with sleep often — trouble falling or staying asleep. This is the piece most worth tending.

bullets: A racing mind at night is usually daytime overflow — what’s unprocessed surfaces once it’s finally quiet.
Sleeplessness responds to a steady wind-down far better than to “trying harder” to fall asleep.
sleeplessness.try: If you’re awake past ~20 minutes, get up, keep lights low, and do something dull until sleepy — keep the bed for sleep, not struggle.

quality.blurb: How restorative your sleep actually feels — deep, unbroken, and leaving you refreshed.

note.low: Your sleep isn’t restoring you much — light or broken nights that don’t leave you refreshed.
note.mid: Your sleep quality is okay but inconsistent — some genuinely good nights, some that don’t land.
note.high: Your sleep is genuinely restorative — deep, refreshing rest. A real asset to lean on.

bullets: Quality beats quantity — seven solid hours rebuilds you more than nine broken ones.
A cool, dark, screen-free room is the single biggest lever on sleep quality.
quality.try: Make the room properly cool and dark tonight — eye mask or blackout — and notice how the morning feels.

rest.blurb: How much genuine rest and downtime you get while awake — not just sleep, but true off-duty time.

note.low: Daytime rest is thin — you’re rarely fully off-duty, so the day never lets up before night.
note.mid: You get some downtime, but it’s patchy — rest that’s often half-attention or interrupted.
note.high: You give yourself real downtime — genuine off-duty pockets that let the system settle.

bullets: Rest while awake sets up rest while asleep — a day with no pauses rarely ends in a calm night.
Real rest is undivided — scrolling counts less than ten quiet minutes with nothing demanded of you.
rest.try: Schedule one genuinely off-duty pocket tomorrow — no phone, no task — and protect it like an appointment.

energy.blurb: How much steady, usable energy you have through the day — the daytime payoff of good rest.

note.low: Daytime energy is running low — you’re pushing through dips rather than feeling genuinely fuelled.
note.mid: Your energy is okay but uneven — decent stretches broken by real slumps.
note.high: Your daytime energy is strong and steady — a clear sign your rest is doing its job.

bullets: Steady energy is the clearest daytime read on whether your nights are working.
Morning light and movement stabilise energy more reliably than caffeine timing.
energy.try: Get a few minutes of daylight within an hour of waking — it anchors your body clock and steadies energy.

winddown.blurb: How consistent and calming your pre-sleep routine is — the runway that tells your body it’s nearly night.

note.low: There’s little wind-down — you go from full-on straight to lights-out, with no runway to land.
note.mid: You have some wind-down, but it’s inconsistent — good some nights, skipped on busy ones.
note.high: You’ve got a real wind-down routine — a consistent runway that eases you toward sleep. Lean on it.

bullets: A predictable runway matters more than a perfect one — the same order of calm steps, most nights.
Dimming lights and screens in the last hour is the strongest cue that night is coming.
winddown.try: Pick a fixed “last hour” ritual — dim lights, no screens, one calm activity — and run it three nights this week.

wiredWorn.name: Wired & Worn
wiredWorn.summary: Trouble sleeping and low recovery — sleep needs real attention.

restlessButResourced.name: Restless but Resourced
restlessButResourced.summary: Sleep is disrupted, but you have recovery habits to build on.

quietButUnderRested.name: Quiet but Under-rested
quietButUnderRested.summary: You fall asleep okay, but you’re not truly recovering.

restedRecharged.name: Rested & Recharged
restedRecharged.summary: Healthy sleep and recovery across the board.

patternDetail.base: Your sleep difficulty reads {{difficulty}}/100, and your recovery resources — quality, rest, energy and wind-down — average {{resource}}/100. {{connector}}the resource most worth building is {{strengthLabel}}.
patternDetail.highDifficulty: Falling and staying asleep is the harder part right now —
patternDetail.lowDifficulty: Getting to sleep isn’t the main issue —

worry.blurb: How often anxious “what if” thoughts show up and how much space they take.

note.low: Worry is staying in proportion — it shows up, but it isn’t running the show.
note.mid: Worry is a frequent visitor — enough to be tiring, though not constant.
note.high: Worry is loud and frequent right now. That’s draining — and it’s also very workable.

bullets: Worry is the mind trying to keep you safe — it over-fires, but it isn’t a flaw or a diagnosis.
Most worries never happen; naming the specific fear shrinks it more than pushing it away.
worry.try: Set a 10-minute “worry window” each day — write the worries down then, and gently postpone them the rest of the day.

rumination.blurb: How much your mind replays past moments or loops on the same thoughts.

note.low: You’re not getting too stuck — thoughts come and go without looping for long.
note.mid: Some looping is happening — replaying conversations or decisions more than helps.
note.high: Your mind is caught in replay a lot. Looping feels productive but rarely resolves anything — that’s the trap.

bullets: Rumination masquerades as problem-solving, but it mostly recycles the problem without moving it.
Shifting the body — a short walk, cold water — breaks a loop faster than thinking your way out.
rumination.try: When you notice the loop, name it (“that’s rumination”) and change your physical state — stand up, move, step outside.

catastrophizing.blurb: How often your mind jumps to the worst-case version of things.

note.low: You’re mostly keeping perspective — the worst-case isn’t your default.
note.mid: The worst-case shows up fairly often, colouring how risky things feel.
note.high: Your mind leaps to the worst-case a lot. It feels like foresight, but it’s the anxiety talking, not the odds.

bullets: Catastrophising overweights the scary outcome and underweights your ability to cope with it.
Asking “what’s most likely?” and “could I handle it?” gently rebalances the picture.
catastrophizing.try: When you catch a worst-case thought, jot the feared outcome, the most likely outcome, and one way you’d cope.

physical.blurb: How much anxiety shows up in your body — tension, racing heart, restlessness, shallow breath.

note.low: Your body is staying fairly settled — anxiety isn’t showing up much physically.
note.mid: There’s some physical tension — a tight chest, restlessness, or shallow breathing at times.
note.high: Anxiety is showing up strongly in your body. Body and mind feed each other — calming one calms the other.

bullets: Physical anxiety is your nervous system on high alert — real and very treatable, not weakness.
Slow exhales are the fastest off-switch: a longer breath out tells the body it’s safe.
physical.try: Breathe in for 4, out for 6, for two minutes. The longer out-breath is what settles the nervous system.

refocus.blurb: How well you can step out of an anxious spiral and bring your attention back.

note.low: This steadying skill is still developing — right now it’s hard to step out once the spiral starts.
note.mid: You can sometimes redirect your attention, though it doesn’t always hold.
note.high: You’re genuinely good at catching the spiral and refocusing. This is your strongest lever — lean on it.

bullets: Noticing “I’m spiralling” and redirecting is the single most protective anxiety skill there is.
It’s a muscle, not a trait — every time you redirect on purpose, it gets a little easier.
refocus.try: Pick one grounding anchor — five things you can see, or your feet on the floor — and use it the moment you notice the spin.

caughtInTheSpin.name: Caught in the Spin
caughtInTheSpin.summary: High anxious thinking with little ability to step out of it — and that ability is buildable.

anxiousButAware.name: Anxious but Aware
anxiousButAware.summary: Strong worry and overthinking, but you can catch and redirect it.

quietlyTense.name: Quietly Tense
quietlyTense.summary: Lower-grade anxiety, but few tools yet to settle it when it rises.

steadyMind.name: Steady Mind
steadyMind.summary: Low anxiety and a good ability to refocus.

patternDetail.base: Your anxious thinking averages {{difficulty}}/100, while your ability to refocus sits at {{resource}}/100. {{connector}}Right now {{heaviest}} is the loudest of the four.
patternDetail.reassuring: The reassuring part: you can already catch and redirect the spiral — that’s the lever everything else turns on.
patternDetail.build: The most useful thing to build is that catch-and-redirect skill — it’s what turns the volume down.

awareness.blurb: How clearly you notice your own emotions, triggers, and patterns as they happen.

note.low: Self-awareness is your biggest area to grow — noticing what you feel, as you feel it, is the foundation everything else builds on.
note.mid: You’re building real self-awareness — you catch your emotions some of the time, and that’s growing.
note.high: You read your own emotions clearly and early. That insight is the engine of every other EI skill.

bullets: Naming an emotion as it arises (“I’m frustrated”) is the single most powerful EI habit — it creates a pause before reaction.
Self-awareness is a skill, not a fixed trait; brief daily check-ins build it surprisingly fast.
awareness.try: Twice a day, pause and name what you’re feeling in one word. No fixing — just noticing.

regulation.blurb: How well you manage strong emotions and respond on purpose rather than on impulse.

note.low: Self-regulation is a key area to build — strong feelings tend to drive the response before you can choose it. Very learnable.
note.mid: You can steady yourself some of the time; under real pressure it’s still a work in progress.
note.high: You handle strong emotions well — you can feel them fully and still choose your response.

bullets: Regulation isn’t suppression — it’s feeling the emotion and still choosing the response.
A slow exhale or a short pause buys the few seconds that turn reaction into choice.
regulation.try: Next time you feel a surge, take one slow breath before responding — that pause is the whole skill.

empathy.blurb: How well you sense and understand what other people are feeling.

note.low: Empathy is an area with real room to grow — tuning into others’ feelings is a skill that strengthens with attention.
note.mid: You pick up on others’ emotions some of the time; it deepens the more you look for it.
note.high: You read others well — you sense what people feel, often before they say it. A genuine strength.

bullets: Empathy starts with curiosity — asking “what might they be feeling?” before responding.
Listening to understand, rather than to reply, is the fastest way to strengthen it.
empathy.try: In one conversation today, focus entirely on understanding the other person’s feeling before you respond.

relationships.blurb: How well you build, maintain, and navigate relationships — including the tricky moments.

note.low: Managing relationships is your biggest growth area — handling tension and connection on purpose is a learnable craft.
note.mid: You manage relationships reasonably well; the harder conversations are where it’s still growing.
note.high: You navigate relationships skilfully — connecting, and handling friction without it derailing things.

bullets: Most relationship skill is repair, not perfection — naming and mending small ruptures keeps trust strong.
Clear, kind directness beats avoidance almost every time.
relationships.try: Have one slightly braver conversation this week — name something kindly that you’d normally let slide.

motivation.blurb: How much internal drive and persistence you bring toward what matters to you.

note.low: Motivation is an area to nurture — reconnecting to what genuinely matters to you is where drive gets rebuilt.
note.mid: Your drive is there but uneven — stronger when the “why” is clear, thinner when it isn’t.
note.high: You’ve got real internal drive — you keep moving toward what matters without needing constant external push.

bullets: Durable motivation comes from meaning, not willpower — a clear “why” outlasts any push.
Small, visible progress feeds drive more than distant goals do.
motivation.try: Write down the one reason your current goal matters to you, and keep it somewhere you’ll see it.

patternDetail.text: Your profile leads with {{dominantLabel}} ({{dominantPct}}%). The highest-leverage place to grow is {{priorityLabel}} ({{priorityPct}}%) — real room to build, not a weakness.

distractibility.blurb: How easily your attention gets pulled away from what you meant to do.

note.low: You hold your focus well — distractions don’t pull you off course much.
note.mid: Distractions catch you fairly often — enough to fragment a focused stretch.
note.high: You’re getting pulled off task a lot. That’s hard on follow-through — and very workable with the right setup.

bullets: Most distraction is environmental, not a willpower failure — the fix is usually the setup, not trying harder.
Every switch back to a task costs minutes of refocus; protecting a clear runway pays off fast.
distractibility.try: Pick one 25-minute block today with your phone in another room and notifications off. One block, fully protected.

procrastination.blurb: How much you delay or avoid starting the things that matter.

note.low: You tend to get started without much delay — avoidance isn’t a big drag on you.
note.mid: Some delaying is happening — tasks slipping or starting later than you’d like.
note.high: Putting things off is a real pattern right now. Starting is the hard part — and the part with the best leverage.

bullets: Procrastination is usually about the feeling a task brings up, not laziness — shrinking the first step shrinks the dread.
Starting is the whole battle; momentum almost always follows the first two minutes.
procrastination.try: Pick the task you’re avoiding and commit to just two minutes on it. Permission to stop after — you usually won’t.

attention.blurb: How well you can hold sustained concentration on one thing over time.

note.low: Sustained attention is the skill most worth building — holding focus over time is hard right now, and it’s trainable.
note.mid: You can concentrate for a while, though it’s not yet a reliable, deep stretch.
note.high: You can sink into deep, sustained focus — a genuine asset for getting real work done.

bullets: Attention is a muscle — focused blocks, even short ones, build the capacity for longer ones.
Single-tasking trains attention; constant switching erodes it.
attention.try: Do one task at a time today, start to finish, before opening anything else. Notice how much further you get.

management.blurb: How well you organise tasks, plan your time, and keep things from piling up.

note.low: Task and time management is an area to build — without a system, things pile up and pull at your attention.
note.mid: You’ve got some structure, but it’s inconsistent — enough slips through to add friction.
note.high: You manage tasks and time well — a clear system that keeps things moving and your head clear.

bullets: A trusted external list frees your mind from holding everything — and that frees up focus.
Planning the day in a couple of minutes beats reacting to it for hours.
management.try: Each morning, write the three things that would make the day a win — and do the hardest first.

motivation.blurb: How much drive and energy you bring to getting things done.

note.low: Drive is running low right now — reconnecting to why the work matters is where energy gets rebuilt.
note.mid: Your motivation is there but uneven — stronger when the goal is clear, thinner when it isn’t.
note.high: You’ve got real drive and energy — a strong engine to point at what matters most.

bullets: Energy follows meaning — a clear “why” sustains effort better than any push.
Visible progress feeds drive; break big goals into wins you can actually see.
motivation.try: Name the one reason your current goal matters, and put it where you’ll see it while you work.

scatteredStalling.name: Scattered & Stalling
scatteredStalling.summary: High distraction and delay with limited focus tools — all of it buildable.

willingButPulled.name: Willing but Pulled
willingButPulled.summary: You have drive and ability, but distraction and delay keep derailing you.

calmButCoasting.name: Calm but Coasting
calmButCoasting.summary: Few distractions, but low focus and drive — easy to drift.

inTheZone.name: In the Zone
inTheZone.summary: Strong focus and follow-through with little friction.

patternDetail.base: Your friction — distraction and delay — averages {{difficulty}}/100, while your focus capability sits at {{resource}}/100. {{connector}}{{heaviest}} is the biggest drag, while {{thinnest}} is the skill most worth building.
patternDetail.frictionHeavier: Right now the friction side is what’s pulling your score down —
patternDetail.capabilityLifting: Your capability is doing the heavy lifting —

stress.headTitle: {{name}}, here’s how stress and burnout are showing up for you.
stress.scoreSentence: Your overall load is {{v}} / 100. This blends all the areas together — problems add to the load, and your strengths take away from it.
stress.patternFoot: Burnout sits in the bottom-right corner — lots of demands and little recovery. The further your dot is from there, the safer you are.
stress.radarCaption: A bigger shape means more stress in that area.
habits: Take one real break each day — a proper lunch away from your screen, or a short walk.
Do the tiring tasks in one block instead of spreading them across the whole day.
Set one limit you can keep: a set finish time, or notifications off after a certain hour.
Each day, name one thing you got to decide for yourself — it helps you feel more in control.
End the day by noting one moment that felt good, however small.
stress.closingBaseline: This is your starting point. There’s nothing to fix today — just one small thing to work on. Come back in 60–90 days and see what changes.
stress.closingReturn: This is one more check-in on your path — watch how these numbers change over time. Change is the proof.

sleep.headTitle: {{name}}, here’s how your sleep and recovery are holding up.
sleep.scoreSentence: Your sleep and recovery score is {{v}} / 100. It blends how easily you sleep with how well you recover. Higher is better.
sleep.patternFoot: The best corner is the top-left — easy sleep and strong recovery. The further your dot is from the bottom-right, the better you’re sleeping.
sleep.radarCaption: A bigger shape means better sleep and recovery in that area.
sleep.legendGreen: green = sleep and recovery (longer is better)
habits: Wake up at the same time every day, weekends too — a steady wake-up time sets the whole rhythm.
Get daylight within an hour of waking up — it helps you feel sleepy earlier at night.
Stop caffeine after early afternoon — it stays in your body longer than you think.
Turn the lights down and put screens away for the last hour — give your brain time to slow down.
If you can’t sleep, get up and reset instead of lying there — keep your bed just for sleep.
sleep.closingBaseline: This is your sleep starting point. Pick one habit above and try it for two weeks — sleep changes slowly at first, then all at once.
sleep.closingReturn: This is one more check-in on your path — watch how these numbers change over time. Change is the proof.

anxiety.headTitle: {{name}}, here’s how anxiety and overthinking are showing up for you.
anxiety.scoreSentence: Your overall load is {{v}} / 100. This blends all the areas — anxious habits add to the load, and your ability to refocus takes away from it.
anxiety.patternFoot: Being “caught in the spin” sits in the bottom-right — lots of anxious thinking with little room to step back. The further your dot is from there, the calmer things are.
anxiety.radarCaption: A bigger shape means more anxiety in that area.
anxiety.legendGreen: green = ability to refocus (longer is better)
habits: Name it to calm it — when anxiety rises, say to yourself “this is worry.” Naming it loosens its hold.
Breathe out slowly when your body tightens — in for 4, out for 6. The long out-breath helps you feel safe.
Give worry a set time — 10 minutes to write it down, so it doesn’t spill into the whole day.
Move when you get stuck — a short walk or changing rooms stops the loop faster than thinking harder.
Come back to your senses — name five things you can see, or feel your feet on the floor — to step out of the spin.
anxiety.closingBaseline: This is your starting point — a snapshot of your habits, not a label. Pick one calming habit above and practise it gently. These numbers change with practice.
anxiety.closingReturn: This is one more check-in on your path — watch how these numbers settle over time. Practice is the proof.

ei.scoreSentence: Your EI score is {{v}} / 100 — the average across your five areas. Higher is stronger.
ei.patternFoot: A few strong areas lift the rest — your growth areas are simply the best place to build next.
ei.radarCaption: A bigger shape means stronger emotional intelligence in that area.
ei.driversTitle: The best areas to grow next
ei.legendGreen: every bar is green — longer is stronger
habits: Name your feeling as it comes up — “I’m anxious,” “I’m excited.” Naming it builds the self-awareness everything else rests on.
Pause before you react — one slow breath turns a snap reaction into a choice.
Get curious about others — ask “what might they be feeling?” before you reply.
Fix small upsets early — a quick, honest “that came out wrong” keeps trust strong.
Remember your why — keep the reason your goals matter somewhere you’ll see it.
ei.closingBaseline: This is your starting point — a map of your strengths and room to grow, not a judgement. Emotional intelligence can be learned; pick one habit above and practise it.
ei.closingReturn: This is one more check-in on your growth — watch these areas climb over time. Practice is the proof.

focus.headTitle: {{name}}, here’s how your focus and productivity are holding up.
focus.scoreSentence: Your focus and productivity score is {{v}} / 100. It blends how little you get distracted with how strong your focus skills are. Higher is better.
focus.patternFoot: The best corner is the top-left — few distractions and strong skills. The further your dot is from the bottom-right, the more you’re in the zone.
focus.radarCaption: A bigger shape means stronger focus and productivity in that area.
habits: Work in short blocks — 25 focused minutes, phone away, one task. Short and clear beats long and scattered.
Start before you feel ready — do just two minutes on the thing you’re avoiding, and the rest follows.
Do one thing at a time — finish it before you open the next. Switching is where focus leaks away.
Plan your day in two minutes — pick the three things that matter and do the hardest first.
Fix your surroundings, not just your willpower — remove the nearest distraction before you start.
focus.closingBaseline: This is your starting point — a snapshot of your habits, not a judgement. Pick one focus habit above and try it for two weeks. These numbers change with practice.
focus.closingReturn: This is one more check-in on your path — watch how these numbers climb over time. Practice is the proof.

chrome.audioPreparingTitle: Your audio plan is still being made
chrome.headNote: A tool to help you understand yourself, made from your answers — not a medical test or diagnosis.
chrome.radarAria: Your score in each of the areas
chrome.leanOnText: This is what’s working well for you right now. Use it on the focus areas above — it’s what makes the rest of the plan stick.
chrome.disclaimer: Daybreak is a tool to help you reflect. It is not a medical test, a diagnosis, or treatment. If you’re struggling, reaching out to a trained professional is a brave and worthwhile next step.

json.headerNote: A self-awareness tool generated from your answers — not a clinical or diagnostic assessment.
json.leanOnText: This is what’s working for you right now. Spend it deliberately on the focus areas above — it’s what makes the rest of the plan stick.
json.disclaimer: Daybreak is a self-reflection tool, not a clinical assessment, diagnosis, or treatment. If you’re struggling, reaching out to a licensed professional is a brave and worthwhile next step.

gen.note: Same answers, same report, every time

demo.anchorText: At {{score}}, this is the area holding you steadiest right now. Your plan builds on it.
demo.focusText: At {{score}}, this is where to start. The first week of your plan is built around it.
demo.closerAreas: A closer look at the four areas
demo.planTitle: Your {{days}}-day plan, built from this report
demo.ebookCardSmall: Your own ebook, made from these scores
demo.talkThroughSmall: An AI helper that has already read this report
demo.takeAgainSmall: See these numbers change — that’s the proof
demo.disclaimer: This report is a tool to help you reflect. It is made from your answers using a fixed way of scoring. It is not a medical test, a diagnosis, or a treatment plan. If you’re struggling, talking to a trained professional is a good and brave next step.

exhaustion.label: Exhaustion
exhaustion.desc: How tired you feel — how much energy is left at the end of the day.

cynicism.label: Cynicism
cynicism.desc: Pulling away from work, people, or things you used to care about.

workload.label: Workload
workload.desc: How heavy your work feels, and whether you can keep it up.

recovery.label: Recovery
recovery.desc: How well rest helps you — whether you feel better after, or still worn out.

control.label: Control
control.desc: How much say you feel you have over how your days go.

sleeplessness.label: Sleeplessness
sleeplessness.desc: Trouble falling asleep or staying asleep at night.

quality.label: Quality
quality.desc: How good your sleep is, not just how long you sleep.

rest.label: Rest
rest.desc: Whether you let yourself take real breaks, or keep going without stopping.

energy.label: Energy
energy.desc: How awake and steady you feel during the day.

winddown.label: Wind-down
winddown.desc: How well you slow down and relax before bed.

worry.label: Worry
worry.desc: How often your mind jumps to what might go wrong.

rumination.label: Rumination
rumination.desc: Getting stuck going over the same thoughts again and again.

catastrophizing.label: Catastrophizing
catastrophizing.desc: Jumping straight to the worst thing that could happen.

physical.label: Physical
physical.desc: How worry shows up in your body — tight muscles, feeling restless, a fast heartbeat.

refocus.label: Refocus
refocus.desc: How well you can stop worrying and come back to the moment.

awareness.label: Awareness
awareness.desc: How well you notice and name what you feel, as it happens.

regulation.label: Regulation
regulation.desc: Staying calm with your feelings — thinking before you react under pressure.

empathy.label: Empathy
empathy.desc: Sensing what other people feel, and letting it guide how you act.

relationships.label: Relationships
relationships.desc: Getting along, working through arguments, and making up with people.

motivation.label: Motivation
motivation.desc: The drive that gets you to start and keep going on what matters.

distractibility.label: Distractibility
distractibility.desc: How easily you get pulled away from what you meant to do.

procrastination.label: Procrastination
procrastination.desc: The gap between meaning to start and actually starting.

attention.label: Attention
attention.desc: Staying focused on one thing without your mind wandering.

management.label: Management
management.desc: How you plan your tasks and time so your effort counts.

meta.title: Daybreak — Your mind has a path
